title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: scSidekick wraps CellChat provide streamlined, reproducible cell-cell communication workflow. Four functions cover full pipeline: functions designed multi-condition studies want ask “interactions pathways gained lost Control Treated?” Note: scSidekick requires CellChat >= 2.1.0 (jinworks/CellChat fork). Install : devtools::install_github("jinworks/CellChat")

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
code: library(scSidekick) library(Seurat) library(SeuratData) library(CellChat) library(dplyr)  data("bmcite") bmcite <- UpdateSeuratObject(bmcite) bmcite <- bmcite %>%   NormalizeData() %>% FindVariableFeatures() %>% ScaleData() %>% RunPCA() dims <- Determine_nDims(bmcite, elbow_plot = FALSE) bmcite <- RunUMAP(bmcite, dims = 1:dims$n_dims) bmcite <- FindNeighbors(bmcite, dims = 1:dims$n_dims) bmcite <- FindClusters(bmcite, resolution = 0.5) bmcite <- PrepObject(bmcite,   variables   = c("lane", "donor", "celltype.l1", "celltype.l2", "seurat_clusters"),   group.by    = "celltype.l1",   split.by    = "donor",   output_dir  = tempdir(),   object_name = "bmcite")

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: RunCellChat() splits Seurat object levels group., builds one CellChat object per level, runs full inference pipeline , saves summary figures. cell.defines cell-type identity used communication inference. group.defines groups run separately (typically conditions). function returns named list CellChat objects, one per level group.. CellChat RDS files saved CellChatObjects/ subdirectory output_dir default.
code: cc_results <- RunCellChat(bmcite,   cell.by    = "celltype.l1",   # cell identity column for communication inference   group.by   = "donor",         # one CellChat run per condition/group   output_dir = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat"))

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: Use groups run subset levels fix comparison order:
code: cc_results <- RunCellChat(bmcite,   cell.by    = "celltype.l1",   group.by   = "Condition",   groups     = c("Control", "Treated"),   output_dir = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat"))

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: Set species = "mouse" use CellChatDB.mouse ligand-receptor database:
code: cc_brain <- RunCellChat(brain,   cell.by    = "seurat_clusters",   group.by   = "Condition",   species    = "mouse",   output_dir = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat"))

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: Pass named character vector colors override palette used chord diagrams network plots. Keys must match levels cell..
code: cluster_cols <- GetColors(bmcite, "celltype.l1")  cc_results <- RunCellChat(bmcite,   cell.by    = "celltype.l1",   group.by   = "Condition",   colors     = cluster_cols,   output_dir = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat"))

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: resume = TRUE checks robj_dir already-completed CellChat .rds files skips groups, resuming run stopped. redo_plots = TRUE reloads saved .rds files regenerates PDFs without re-running CellChat inference - useful updating colors fixing cell-type names RenameCellTypeInCC.
code: # Resume an interrupted run cc_results <- RunCellChat(bmcite,   cell.by    = "celltype.l1",   group.by   = "Condition",   output_dir = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat"),   resume     = TRUE)  # Regenerate all PDFs from saved RDS (no re-inference) cc_results <- RunCellChat(bmcite,   cell.by    = "celltype.l1",   group.by   = "Condition",   output_dir = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat"),   redo_plots = TRUE)

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: run.patterns = TRUE (default) additionally runs identifyCommunicationPatterns extract global signaling patterns (incoming outgoing) using NMF. pattern_k_range controls values k tested; best k selected automatically.
code: # Skip pattern analysis to save time cc_results <- RunCellChat(bmcite,   cell.by        = "celltype.l1",   group.by       = "Condition",   output_dir     = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat"),   run.patterns   = FALSE)  # Custom k range cc_results <- RunCellChat(bmcite,   cell.by        = "celltype.l1",   group.by       = "Condition",   output_dir     = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat"),   run.patterns   = TRUE,   pattern_k_range = 2:6)

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: level group., RunCellChat() saves following files:

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: CompareCellChat() takes named list CellChat objects (one per condition) generates comparative visualizations side side. Pain point fixed. Two things go wrong natively. (1) signaling pathway detected one condition absent another, per-pathway plot errors conditions lack , panels silently dropped absence becomes invisible. (2) object draws cell groups colors order, panels comparable position--position. CompareCellChat() puts every condition shared node layout colors group order every panel, every pathway present object. Use thresh = 1 also compare pathways active L-R level reach pathway-level significance conditions.

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
code: # cc_results is the named list returned by RunCellChat() CompareCellChat(   cellchat_list = cc_results,   output_dir    = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat", "Comparison"))

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: previously ran RunCellChat() different session, reload .rds files build list manually:
code: cc_ctrl    <- readRDS(file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat", "CellChatObjects",                                 "Control_CellChat.rds")) cc_treated <- readRDS(file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat", "CellChatObjects",                                 "Treated_CellChat.rds"))  CompareCellChat(   cellchat_list = list(Control = cc_ctrl, Treated = cc_treated),   output_dir    = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat", "Comparison"))

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: RankCellChatPathways() goes one level deeper CompareCellChat: ranks pathways much information flow changes across conditions, decomposes changes cell types driving senders receivers. Four outputs: Flow heatmap - top N pathways x conditions, row-normalized color shows condition relatively higher activity Sender tile plot - faceted pathway; shows much cell type sends per condition Receiver tile plot - receivers Top changed links heatmap - sender-receiver pairs highest variance across conditions, showing exactly cell-type interactions drive pathway-level changes CSV - underlying metrics custom analysis

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
code: rank_results <- RankCellChatPathways(   cellchat_list = cc_results,   output_dir    = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat", "Ranked"))

what: 2. Controlling which pathways and links to show
title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: top_n controls many pathways appear flow heatmap tile plots. top_links controls many sender-receiver pairs appear links heatmap. ranked differential activity (rank_by = "range" "cv").
code: rank_results <- RankCellChatPathways(   cellchat_list = cc_results,   output_dir    = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat", "Ranked"),   top_n         = 30L,   top_links     = 20L,   rank_by       = "cv")    # coefficient of variation instead of range

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: Supply condition colors (flow heatmap strips) cell-type colors (sender/receiver tile annotation strips):
code: cond_colors <- c(Control = "#4575b4", Treated = "#d73027") cell_colors <- GetColors(bmcite, "celltype.l1")  rank_results <- RankCellChatPathways(   cellchat_list = cc_results,   output_dir    = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat", "Ranked"),   colors        = cond_colors,   cell_colors   = cell_colors)

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
code: # Data frame: pathway x condition flow + ranking columns head(rank_results$flow_table)  # Long data frame: which cell types send each pathway per condition head(rank_results$sender_table)  # Long data frame: which cell types receive each pathway per condition head(rank_results$receiver_table)  # Long data frame: sender-receiver pair flow per condition head(rank_results$links_table)  # ggplot2 / pheatmap objects rank_results$flow_heatmap rank_results$sender_plot rank_results$receiver_plot rank_results$links_plot

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: rename cluster cell type running CellChat (e.g. correct annotation mistake), RenameCellTypeInCC() patches CellChat object new name propagates internal arrays: probability matrices, centrality scores, communication pattern tables, LR significance tables. Communication probabilities recomputed - labels change. safe underlying values invariant cell type called.

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
code: cc_fixed <- RenameCellTypeInCC(cc_ctrl,   old_name = "Immune_contam",   new_name  = "NK_TCells")  # Save the fixed object back saveRDS(cc_fixed,   file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat", "CellChatObjects", "Control_CellChat.rds"))

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: typical workflow: patch re-save RDS files, regenerate comparison figures without re-inference.
code: # Step 1: patch and re-save all RDS files rds_dir   <- file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat", "CellChatObjects") rds_files <- list.files(rds_dir, pattern = "CellChat\\.rds$", full.names = TRUE)  for (f in rds_files) {   cc <- RenameCellTypeInCC(readRDS(f),     old_name = "Immune_contam",     new_name  = "NK_TCells")   saveRDS(cc, f) }  # Step 2: reload the fixed list and regenerate comparison figures cclist <- setNames(   lapply(rds_files, readRDS),   sub("_CellChat\\.rds$", "", basename(rds_files)) )  CompareCellChat(cclist,   output_dir = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat", "Comparison"))  RankCellChatPathways(cclist,   output_dir = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat", "Ranked"))  # Step 3: regenerate per-group RunCellChat PDFs (plots only, no re-inference) RunCellChat(bmcite,   cell.by    = "celltype.l1",   group.by   = "Condition",   output_dir = file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat"),   redo_plots = TRUE)

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
code: out_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "CellChat")  # Step 1: run CellChat per condition cc_results <- RunCellChat(bmcite,   cell.by    = "celltype.l1",   group.by   = "Condition",   groups     = c("Control", "Treated"),   colors     = GetColors(bmcite, "celltype.l1"),   output_dir = out_dir,   resume     = TRUE)  # Step 2: cross-condition comparison CompareCellChat(   cellchat_list = cc_results,   output_dir    = file.path(out_dir, "Comparison"))  # Step 3: pathway ranking RankCellChatPathways(   cellchat_list = cc_results,   output_dir    = file.path(out_dir, "Ranked"),   top_n         = 20L)

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
text: Vignette 01: PrepObject - register cell-type colors used chord diagrams Vignette 06: CellTypeAssignmentHelper - assign cell types CellChat Vignette 08: RunGSEA - pathway-level gene set enrichment Vignette 09: RunGSEA_pseudobulk - sample-level pseudobulk pathway analysis

title: scSidekick - Cell-Cell Communication: CellChat Suite
code: sessionInfo()

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: fancy analysis, usually just want understand object: many cells per sample, cell-type composition shifts conditions, donors carry clinical features. base-R answer pile nested table() calls dozen lines dplyr + ggplot2. scSidekick turns one-liners: SummarizeMetadata() - collapse giant meta.data tidy per-group summary table (counts, numeric means, categorical breakdowns). PlotMetaSummary() - composition bar charts (counts percentages) across one grouping levels, single call. PlotAlluvial(), PlotRose(), PlotChord(), PlotAtlasWheel() - family “glance” composition graphics flows, proportions, relationships, whole-atlas overviews. use bmcite, bone-marrow CITE-seq dataset (~30 k cells) rich metadata: lane, donor, celltype.l1, celltype.l2.
code: library(scSidekick) library(Seurat) library(SeuratData) library(ggforce)   # PlotAlluvial uses ggforce's parallel-sets stats

what: 1. SummarizeMetadata() - one tidy table from messy metadata
title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: Point SummarizeMetadata() one ID columns returns one row per ID combination : n_cells count, mean every numeric column, compact breakdown every categorical column - columns didn’t name summarised automatically. Numeric columns aggregated numeric_func (default mean; pass median, max, custom function, …). Use several ID columns summarise finer grain: everyday “’s actually object?” answer - scales: multi-million-cell atlas can collapse one row per donor seconds.
code: donor_summary <- SummarizeMetadata(bmcite, id_columns = "donor") donor_summary[, 1:5]   # first few summary columns ## # A tibble: 2 × 5 ##   donor  n_cells nCount_RNA nFeature_RNA nCount_ADT ##   <fct>    <int>      <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl> ## 1 batch1   14468      2779.         858.      4588. ## 2 batch2   16204      3116.         912.      4617. ds <- SummarizeMetadata(bmcite,                         id_columns   = c("donor", "celltype.l1"),                         numeric_func = median) head(ds[, c("donor", "celltype.l1", "n_cells", "nFeature_RNA")]) ## # A tibble: 6 × 4 ##   donor  celltype.l1      n_cells nFeature_RNA ##   <fct>  <fct>              <int>        <dbl> ## 1 batch1 B cell              1700         668  ## 2 batch1 Mono/DC             3845         838  ## 3 batch1 NK                   615         715  ## 4 batch1 Progenitor cells    1518        1394. ## 5 batch1 T cell              6790         701  ## 6 batch2 B cell              2053         710

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: common request - cell-type composition differ samples? - one call. variables ’re counting, fill_variable colours stack, count_unit = "cells" counts cells (vs donors).  Add percent = TRUE normalised composition - ideal group sizes differ:
code: PlotMetaSummary(bmcite,   variables     = c("celltype.l1", "celltype.l2"),   fill_variable = "donor",   count_unit    = "cells") PlotMetaSummary(bmcite,   variables     = c("celltype.l1", "celltype.l2"),   fill_variable = "donor",   count_unit    = "cells",   percent       = TRUE)

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: clinical datasets usually want per-donor counts, per-cell. Setting id_column collapses cells donors first, lays variables - row_variable facets rows. exactly ’d summarise large patient cohort (e.g. Braak / APOE / CERAD sex): function call handles 30 k-cell demo 1.8 M-cell, 150-column clinical atlas - just collapses unit ask .
code: # Illustrative - for a clinical atlas `md` with one row per cell: PlotMetaSummary(md,   id_column     = "Donor.ID",   variables     = c("Braak", "APOE.Genotype", "CERAD.score"),   fill_variable = "Cognitive.Status",   row_variable  = "Sex")

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: Bar charts aren’t always clearest view. scSidekick ships four complementary “glance” graphics.

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: Alluvial plots show cells flow two categorical axes - great “donors contribute cell types”.
code: PlotAlluvial(md,   axes = c("donor", "celltype.l1"))

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: polar/“rose” chart composition per group - compact alternative stacked bars.
code: PlotRose(md,   stat.by = "celltype.l1",   group.by = "donor")

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: Chord diagrams emphasise pairing two categoricals (, cell types appear donors). PlotChord() uses circlize hood.
code: PlotChord(md,   var1 = "donor",   var2 = "celltype.l1")

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: multi-study atlases, PlotAtlasWheel() draws single “cover figure” summarising cells per group across studies patients - counts centre, wheel wedges around . (Shown template; needs study/patient columns bmcite doesn’t carry.)
code: PlotAtlasWheel(meta,   group.by = "CellType",   study_by   = "Study",   patient_by = "Patient.ID",   title      = "My Atlas",   cell_label = "Cells")

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: Atlas-scale objects (hundreds thousands millions cells) break usual “load everything memory” assumption. scSidekick built scale two complementary ways: Streaming heavy functions - feature plots, pseudobulk aggregation, pathway scoring read BPCells / -disk Sketch assays lazily. genes (cells) ask pulled disk, one-liners run 2-million-cell object without materialising . Aggregating tractable unit - collapse millions cells per-sample pseudobulk profiles, analyse fast, robust, sample-level statistics.
code: library(scSidekick) library(Seurat) library(SeuratData)

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: BPCells-backed object count matrix lives disk, RAM. scSidekick’s plotting aggregation functions detect lazy matrix stream - subset requested rows materialising, memory stays flat regardless object size.
code: library(BPCells)  # Write a 10x matrix to a BPCells on-disk directory (once): mat <- open_matrix_10x_hdf5("filtered_feature_bc_matrix.h5") write_matrix_dir(mat, dir = "bp_counts")  # Build a Seurat object whose counts are the on-disk matrix: counts <- open_matrix_dir("bp_counts") obj    <- CreateSeuratObject(counts) |>   NormalizeData() |>   FindVariableFeatures() |>   ScaleData() |>   RunPCA() |>   RunUMAP(dims = 1:30)  obj <- PrepObject(obj,   variables  = c("Sample", "Group", "seurat_clusters"),   group.by   = "seurat_clusters",   output_dir = "./Figures")  # These behave exactly as on an in-memory object - but only the requested # genes are read off disk for each panel: PlotFeaturePlots(obj, features = c("CD3E", "LYZ", "NKG7"), split.by = "Sample") GroupHeatmap(obj, features = marker_genes)

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: interactive work millions cells, Seurat v5’s SketchData() keeps representative subset “memory” full data stays disk. scSidekick functions work either assay - sketch fast iteration, full final figure.
code: obj <- SketchData(obj, ncells = 50000, method = "LeverageScore",                   sketched.assay = "sketch")  # Iterate fast on the sketch assay ... DefaultAssay(obj) <- "sketch" PlotFeaturePlots(obj, features = "CD3E", split.by = "Sample")  # ... then project back to the full dataset for the publication figure. # (For pseudobulk / pathway analysis on sketched objects, pass assay = "RNA" #  explicitly so aggregation uses the full data, not the sketch.)

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: Pseudobulk robust way analyse large multi-sample studies: sum (average) gene within sample, treat samples replicates. ComputePseudobulk() streams matrix (BPCells-safe) caches result object; PlotPseudoBulk() draws per-gene, per-group profiles one point per donor.  samples can add cross-group statistics directly:
code: bmcite <- ComputePseudobulk(bmcite,   group.by = c("donor", "celltype.l1"),   genes    = c("LYZ", "CD3D", "NKG7", "CD79A", "S100A8"),   verbose  = FALSE) PlotPseudoBulk(bmcite,   features = c("LYZ", "CD3D"),   group.by = "celltype.l1",   donor.by = "donor") PlotPseudoBulk(obj,   features    = "APOE",   group.by = "Cognitive.Status",   donor.by = "Donor.ID",   split.by = "CellType",   exclude     = list(Cognitive.Status = "Reference"),   add_stats   = TRUE,   ref_group   = "No dementia",   min_cells   = 10)

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: Two routes, designed big objects:

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: Pseudobulk -> per-contrast differential expression -> GSEA Hallmark / KEGG / Reactome / WikiPathways, plus optional ssGSEA. Sample-level statistics mean result isn’t inflated cell count.
code: RunGSEA_pseudobulk(obj,   identity_column = "CellType",   sample_column   = "Donor.ID",   group_column    = "Cognitive.Status",   contrasts       = list(c("Dementia", "No dementia")),   species         = "human",   output_dir      = "./Figures/Pathways_pseudobulk")

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: want pathway score per cell (e.g. overlay UMAP), RunSCssGSEA() computes single-cell ssGSEA BPCells/sketch-aware (use assay = "RNA" / "sketch" appropriate).
code: RunSCssGSEA(obj,   group.by         = "CellType",   gene_set_library = "H",          # MSigDB Hallmark   output_dir       = "./Figures/ssGSEA")

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: Keep counts disk BPCells; scSidekick streams . Use sketch assay interactive iteration, full assay final figures - pass assay = "RNA" explicitly pseudobulk/pathway functions aggregation uses full data. Prefer pseudobulk (ComputePseudobulk -> PlotPseudoBulk, RunGSEA_pseudobulk) differential pathway analysis across samples - ’s faster statistically sounder per-cell tests.

title: scSidekick - Reporting & Utilities
text: scSidekick built write-writes . analyse, package captures provenance ’ll need months later paper time: Auto-logged figure legends - every figure-saving function drops plain-text .legend sidecar next figure, describing exactly plotted. ExtractMethods() - turns Seurat command log ready--paste methods paragraph. Passive session logging - start_session_logger() records commands plots; summarize_r_session() turns messy session clean report tidy R script. create_analysis_pptx() - assembles saved figures sectioned PowerPoint deck one call. Plus everyday utilities: GetColors(), ShowColors(), theme_NourMin(), Nour_pal(), PlotTrendLabeled(), PlotComposition().

what: 1. ExtractMethods() - a methods paragraph for free
title: scSidekick - Reporting & Utilities
text: ExtractMethods() reads Seurat command log (bmcite@commands) reconstructs key analysis steps prose paragraph structured summary - reverse-engineering pipeline submission time. companion $summary structured list detected steps parameters, handy supplementary table.
code: methods_out <- ExtractMethods(bmcite, cite_seurat = TRUE) cat(methods_out$methods_text) ## Single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) data (30,672 cells, 17,009 genes; assay: RNA) were analyzed using Seurat. Gene expression counts were normalized using LogNormalize (scale factor 10,000) and 2,000 highly variable genes were identified using the vst method. PCA was performed (50 PCs computed). Cells were clustered using the Louvain algorithm at resolution 0.50, yielding 15 clusters. (Cite: Hao et al., Cell 2021 / Hao et al., Nature Methods 2024 as appropriate.)

title: scSidekick - Reporting & Utilities
text: Every scSidekick function saves figure also writes .legend text file beside , generated exact arguments used. never remember figure showed. can also log legends analysis parameters log_figure_legend() log_analysis_params() build custom figure outside package.
code: fig_dir <- file.path(tempdir(), "figs") dir.create(fig_dir, showWarnings = FALSE)  # Save a feature map; a PDF and a matching .legend are written: invisible(GenerateFeatureMaps(bmcite,   features    = "CD3E",   output_dir  = fig_dir,   object_name = "bmcite"))  list.files(fig_dir) ## [1] "CD3E featuremap bmcite.legend" "CD3E featuremap bmcite.pdf" # Read back the auto-generated legend: legend_file <- list.files(fig_dir, pattern = "\\.legend$", full.names = TRUE) cat(readLines(legend_file), sep = "\n") ## UMAP feature plot showing expression of CD3E in bmcite. Colour scale: low (dark blue) to high (dark red), capped at the per-gene maximum (3.89). Each panel represents one group level; legend shows the shared colour bar.

title: scSidekick - Reporting & Utilities
text: start_session_logger() installs lightweight hook records commands run plots draw rest session. Afterwards, summarize_r_session() distills log Markdown report clean, runnable R script - turning exploratory session reproducible output. logger_log_file() logger_plot_dir() tell active log captured plots live.
code: # At the top of an analysis session: start_session_logger()  # ... do your analysis (every command and plot is captured) ...  # At the end, generate a report + a tidy script: summarize_r_session(   output_md = "analysis_report.md",   output_r  = "analysis_clean.R")

title: scSidekick - Reporting & Utilities
text: create_analysis_pptx() scans output_dir saved PDF/PNG figures, groups sections filename keyword, writes .pptx - whole analysis deck, one call. Section grouping rules (keyword filename -> section label): Include exclude sections name, control whether .legend sidecars appended speaker notes slide:
code: create_analysis_pptx(   output_dir  = "./bmcite_Figures",   object_name = "bmcite") create_analysis_pptx(   output_dir       = "./bmcite_Figures",   object_name      = "bmcite",   include_sections = c("Dimensionality Reduction", "Gene Expression"),   include_legends  = TRUE,       # append .legend text as slide speaker notes   legend_font_size = 10)         # font size for legend text in speaker notes

title: scSidekick - Reporting & Utilities
text: GetColors() returns named color vector variable, ready pass scale_color_manual() scSidekick colors argument.
code: ct_cols <- GetColors(bmcite, "celltype.l1") ct_cols ##           B cell          Mono/DC               NK Progenitor cells  ##        "#003F5C"        "#74599E"        "#E46388"        "#F77B38"  ##           T cell  ##        "#8DB032" all_cols <- GetColors(bmcite) names(all_cols) ## [1] "lane"            "donor"           "celltype.l1"     "celltype.l2"     ## [5] "seurat_clusters"

title: scSidekick - Reporting & Utilities
code: ShowColors(bmcite)

title: scSidekick - Reporting & Utilities
text: PlotTrendLabeled() publication-ready composition figure percentage labels; PlotComposition() idea without directional labels.
code: PlotTrendLabeled(bmcite,   stat.by    = "celltype.l1",   group.by   = "lane",   split.by   = "donor",   label.side = "right") PlotComposition(bmcite,   group.by = "celltype.l1",   split.by = "donor")

title: scSidekick - Reporting & Utilities
text: scSidekick ships two hand-curated palettes unified accessor: Nour18 - 18 qualitative colors (blues, purples, pinks, reds, oranges, greens). Best datasets 18 cell types. Nour20 - 20 colors sweeping full visible spectrum. Better atlases many fine-grained subtypes. Nour_pal() - returns color-function (like scale_color_brewer internally), can pull number colors either palette name.   Nour_pal() pulls subset either palette name plugs straight scale_color_manual() / scale_fill_manual():  SelectColors() lets interactively pick subset colors name Nour18 - useful want hand-pick specific hues figure:
code: # Nour18 - 18 qualitative colors scales::show_col(Nour18, cex_label = 0.6) # Nour20 - full-spectrum 20-color palette scales::show_col(Nour20, cex_label = 0.6) df <- data.frame(x = 1:8, y = 1, grp = LETTERS[1:8]) ggplot(df, aes(x, y, color = grp)) +   geom_point(size = 6) +   scale_color_manual(values = Nour_pal("main")(8)) +   theme_NourMin() # Returns the hex codes for just the colors you name SelectColors("blue1", "teal", "pink2", "orange")

title: scSidekick - Reporting & Utilities
text: Seurat clusters come 0, 1, 2, .... RenameClusters() converts zero-padded, prefixed labels (C01, C02, …) axes sort correctly look publication-ready. Common usage right FindClusters():
code: # Simulate raw Seurat cluster IDs raw_clusters <- factor(c(0, 1, 2, 0, 3, 1, 2))  RenameClusters(raw_clusters, prefix = "C", start = 1) ##  0  1  2  0  3  1  2  ## C1 C2 C3 C1 C4 C2 C3  ## Levels: C1 C2 C3 C4 bmcite$Cluster <- RenameClusters(bmcite$seurat_clusters, prefix = "C", start = 1) # seurat cluster 0 -> "C01", cluster 1 -> "C02", etc. # factor levels are in cluster order, so ggplot axes always sort correctly.  bmcite <- PrepObject(bmcite, variables = "Cluster", group.by = "Cluster") PlotDimPlots(bmcite, group.by = "Cluster")

title: scSidekick - Reporting & Utilities
text: Vignette 01: PrepObject, ShowColors, GetColors - color management Vignette 13: PlotMetaSummary() composition chart family Every analysis vignette saves output_dir - feeds straight create_analysis_pptx() produces .legend sidecars automatically.

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: fancy analysis, usually just want understand object: many cells per sample, cell-type composition shifts conditions, donors carry clinical features. base-R answer pile nested table() calls dozen lines dplyr + ggplot2. scSidekick turns one-liners: SummarizeMetadata() - collapse giant meta.data tidy per-group summary table (counts, numeric means, categorical breakdowns). PlotMetaSummary() - composition bar charts (counts percentages) across one grouping levels, single call. PlotAlluvial(), PlotRose(), PlotChord(), PlotAtlasWheel() - family “glance” composition graphics flows, proportions, relationships, whole-atlas overviews. use bmcite, bone-marrow CITE-seq dataset (~30 k cells) rich metadata: lane, donor, celltype.l1, celltype.l2.
code: library(scSidekick) library(Seurat) library(SeuratData) library(ggforce)   # PlotAlluvial uses ggforce's parallel-sets stats

what: 1. SummarizeMetadata() - one tidy table from messy metadata
title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: Point SummarizeMetadata() one ID columns returns one row per ID combination : n_cells count, mean every numeric column, compact breakdown every categorical column - columns didn’t name summarised automatically. Numeric columns aggregated numeric_func (default mean; pass median, max, custom function, …). Use several ID columns summarise finer grain: everyday “’s actually object?” answer - scales: multi-million-cell atlas can collapse one row per donor seconds.
code: donor_summary <- SummarizeMetadata(bmcite, id_columns = "donor") donor_summary[, 1:5]   # first few summary columns ## # A tibble: 2 × 5 ##   donor  n_cells nCount_RNA nFeature_RNA nCount_ADT ##   <fct>    <int>      <dbl>        <dbl>      <dbl> ## 1 batch1   14468      2779.         858.      4588. ## 2 batch2   16204      3116.         912.      4617. ds <- SummarizeMetadata(bmcite,                         id_columns   = c("donor", "celltype.l1"),                         numeric_func = median) head(ds[, c("donor", "celltype.l1", "n_cells", "nFeature_RNA")]) ## # A tibble: 6 × 4 ##   donor  celltype.l1      n_cells nFeature_RNA ##   <fct>  <fct>              <int>        <dbl> ## 1 batch1 B cell              1700         668  ## 2 batch1 Mono/DC             3845         838  ## 3 batch1 NK                   615         715  ## 4 batch1 Progenitor cells    1518        1394. ## 5 batch1 T cell              6790         701  ## 6 batch2 B cell              2053         710

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: common request - cell-type composition differ samples? - one call. variables ’re counting, fill_variable colours stack, count_unit = "cells" counts cells (vs donors).  Add percent = TRUE normalised composition - ideal group sizes differ:
code: PlotMetaSummary(bmcite,   variables     = c("celltype.l1", "celltype.l2"),   fill_variable = "donor",   count_unit    = "cells") PlotMetaSummary(bmcite,   variables     = c("celltype.l1", "celltype.l2"),   fill_variable = "donor",   count_unit    = "cells",   percent       = TRUE)

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: clinical datasets usually want per-donor counts, per-cell. Setting id_column collapses cells donors first, lays variables - row_variable facets rows. exactly ’d summarise large patient cohort (e.g. Braak / APOE / CERAD sex): function call handles 30 k-cell demo 1.8 M-cell, 150-column clinical atlas - just collapses unit ask .
code: # Illustrative - for a clinical atlas `md` with one row per cell: PlotMetaSummary(md,   id_column     = "Donor.ID",   variables     = c("Braak", "APOE.Genotype", "CERAD.score"),   fill_variable = "Cognitive.Status",   row_variable  = "Sex")

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: Bar charts aren’t always clearest view. scSidekick ships four complementary “glance” graphics.

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: Alluvial plots show cells flow two categorical axes - great “donors contribute cell types”.
code: PlotAlluvial(md,   axes = c("donor", "celltype.l1"))

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: polar/“rose” chart composition per group - compact alternative stacked bars.
code: PlotRose(md,   stat.by = "celltype.l1",   group.by = "donor")

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: Chord diagrams emphasise pairing two categoricals (, cell types appear donors). PlotChord() uses circlize hood.
code: PlotChord(md,   var1 = "donor",   var2 = "celltype.l1")

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: multi-study atlases, PlotAtlasWheel() draws single “cover figure” summarising cells per group across studies patients - counts centre, wheel wedges around . (Shown template; needs study/patient columns bmcite doesn’t carry.)
code: PlotAtlasWheel(meta,   group.by = "CellType",   study_by   = "Study",   patient_by = "Patient.ID",   title      = "My Atlas",   cell_label = "Cells")

title: scSidekick - Exploring Metadata & Composition
text: Vignette 01: PrepObject() - register colours charts reuse Vignette 03: PlotComposition() / PlotTrendLabeled() - composition trends UMAP Vignette 14: large-data workflows (BPCells / sketch / pseudobulk) summarising massive objects

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: Atlas-scale objects (hundreds thousands millions cells) break usual “load everything memory” assumption. scSidekick built scale two complementary ways: Streaming heavy functions - feature plots, pseudobulk aggregation, pathway scoring read BPCells / -disk Sketch assays lazily. genes (cells) ask pulled disk, one-liners run 2-million-cell object without materialising . Aggregating tractable unit - collapse millions cells per-sample pseudobulk profiles, analyse fast, robust, sample-level statistics.
code: library(scSidekick) library(Seurat) library(SeuratData)

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: BPCells-backed object count matrix lives disk, RAM. scSidekick’s plotting aggregation functions detect lazy matrix stream - subset requested rows materialising, memory stays flat regardless object size.
code: library(BPCells)  # Write a 10x matrix to a BPCells on-disk directory (once): mat <- open_matrix_10x_hdf5("filtered_feature_bc_matrix.h5") write_matrix_dir(mat, dir = "bp_counts")  # Build a Seurat object whose counts are the on-disk matrix: counts <- open_matrix_dir("bp_counts") obj    <- CreateSeuratObject(counts) |>   NormalizeData() |>   FindVariableFeatures() |>   ScaleData() |>   RunPCA() |>   RunUMAP(dims = 1:30)  obj <- PrepObject(obj,   variables  = c("Sample", "Group", "seurat_clusters"),   group.by   = "seurat_clusters",   output_dir = "./Figures")  # These behave exactly as on an in-memory object - but only the requested # genes are read off disk for each panel: PlotFeaturePlots(obj, features = c("CD3E", "LYZ", "NKG7"), split.by = "Sample") GroupHeatmap(obj, features = marker_genes)

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: interactive work millions cells, Seurat v5’s SketchData() keeps representative subset “memory” full data stays disk. scSidekick functions work either assay - sketch fast iteration, full final figure.
code: obj <- SketchData(obj, ncells = 50000, method = "LeverageScore",                   sketched.assay = "sketch")  # Iterate fast on the sketch assay ... DefaultAssay(obj) <- "sketch" PlotFeaturePlots(obj, features = "CD3E", split.by = "Sample")  # ... then project back to the full dataset for the publication figure. # (For pseudobulk / pathway analysis on sketched objects, pass assay = "RNA" #  explicitly so aggregation uses the full data, not the sketch.)

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: Pseudobulk robust way analyse large multi-sample studies: sum (average) gene within sample, treat samples replicates. ComputePseudobulk() streams matrix (BPCells-safe) caches result object; PlotPseudoBulk() draws per-gene, per-group profiles one point per donor.  samples can add cross-group statistics directly:
code: bmcite <- ComputePseudobulk(bmcite,   group.by = c("donor", "celltype.l1"),   genes    = c("LYZ", "CD3D", "NKG7", "CD79A", "S100A8"),   verbose  = FALSE) PlotPseudoBulk(bmcite,   features = c("LYZ", "CD3D"),   group.by = "celltype.l1",   donor.by = "donor") PlotPseudoBulk(obj,   features    = "APOE",   group.by = "Cognitive.Status",   donor.by = "Donor.ID",   split.by = "CellType",   exclude     = list(Cognitive.Status = "Reference"),   add_stats   = TRUE,   ref_group   = "No dementia",   min_cells   = 10)

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: Two routes, designed big objects:

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: Pseudobulk -> per-contrast differential expression -> GSEA Hallmark / KEGG / Reactome / WikiPathways, plus optional ssGSEA. Sample-level statistics mean result isn’t inflated cell count.
code: RunGSEA_pseudobulk(obj,   group.by    = "CellType",   sample.by   = "Donor.ID",   contrast.by = "Cognitive.Status",   contrasts   = list(Dementia_vs_Control = "Dementia - No dementia"),   species     = "human",   output_dir  = "./Figures/Pathways_pseudobulk")

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: want pathway score per cell (e.g. overlay UMAP), RunSCssGSEA() computes single-cell ssGSEA BPCells/sketch-aware (use assay = "RNA" / "sketch" appropriate).
code: RunSCssGSEA(obj,   group.by         = "CellType",   gene_set_library = "H",          # MSigDB Hallmark   output_dir       = "./Figures/ssGSEA")

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: Keep counts disk BPCells; scSidekick streams . Use sketch assay interactive iteration, full assay final figures - pass assay = "RNA" explicitly pseudobulk/pathway functions aggregation uses full data. Prefer pseudobulk (ComputePseudobulk -> PlotPseudoBulk, RunGSEA_pseudobulk) differential pathway analysis across samples - ’s faster statistically sounder per-cell tests.

title: scSidekick - Large Datasets: BPCells, Sketch & Pseudobulk
text: Vignette 08: RunGSEA() - cell-level pathway analysis (keyword search, leading-edge visualization) Vignette 09: RunGSEA_pseudobulk() - full pseudobulk GSEA limma-voom Vignette 13: PlotMetaSummary() - donor-level summaries large cohorts Vignette 04: PlotFeaturePlots() - streaming feature-map function

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: scSidekick provides complete Kaplan-Meier survival analysis pipeline bulk RNA-seq expression data. Four functions cover full workflow: Two input modes: TCGA mode - pass tcga_project = "TCGA-GBM" data downloaded, processed, cached automatically. Subsequent calls load instantly cache. Custom matrix mode - pass expr_mat (genes x samples) meta (samples x variables). Works bulk RNA-seq microarray cohort.

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
code: # Core survival packages install.packages(c("survival", "survminer"))  # For TCGA downloads (recommended backend - single consolidated download) BiocManager::install("recount3")  # For richer clinical metadata and molecular subtypes (optional but recommended) BiocManager::install("TCGAbiolinks")

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: LoadTCGA() fetches full expression matrix clinical metadata TCGA TARGET project caches result locally. default backend recount3, downloads single consolidated file per project - much faster reliable per-patient GDC downloads. returned list contains: - $expr_mat - numeric matrix, genes x samples, log2(TPM + 1) - $meta - data frame clinical metadata, one row per sample - $project, $data_type, $backend - provenance fields
code: library(scSidekick)  # Download TCGA-GBM (glioblastoma) - ~173 patients # First call downloads; subsequent calls load from cache instantly gbm <- LoadTCGA("TCGA-GBM")  # Access the expression matrix (genes x samples, log2 TPM+1) dim(gbm$expr_mat)  # Access clinical metadata head(gbm$meta[, c("OS", "status", "gender")])

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: Pass "TCGA-<CODE>" string tcga_project. Common cancer types: Pediatric TARGET projects: "TARGET-AML", "TARGET-NBL", "TARGET-OS", "TARGET-WT", "TARGET-RT", "TARGET-CCSK".

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
code: # Re-download and update the cache gbm <- LoadTCGA("TCGA-GBM", force_reload = TRUE)

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: older TCGA accessions external microarray cohorts, set data_type = "microarray". automatically switches TCGAbiolinks backend (recount3 carries RNA-seq).
code: gbm_array <- LoadTCGA("TCGA-GBM", data_type = "microarray")

what: 2.1 Add GDC clinical and molecular subtype columns
title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: recount3 download includes basic clinical columns (OS, status, gender). EnrichTCGAMeta() adds full GDC comprehensive clinical table TCGA marker-paper molecular subtype columns (paper_*) without re-downloading expression matrix. Available paper_* columns depend cancer type. GBM/LGG typically include paper_IDH.status, paper_Histology, paper_Pan.Glioma.RNA.Expression.Cluster, paper_CIMP.status.
code: # Add IDH status, PAM50, CIMP, stage, age, treatment columns, etc. gbm <- EnrichTCGAMeta(gbm)  # Now $meta contains paper_IDH.status, paper_Histology, etc. SurvMetaSummary(gbm$meta)

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: Always run SurvMetaSummary() SurvPlot() see metadata columns available, look like survival endpoints, good candidates faceting. output organized sections: Survival time columns - candidates time_col Survival event columns - candidates status_col Good facet columns (2-10 unique values, < 50% NA) - candidates facet_row / facet_col Many-level columns - consider binning faceting High-missingness columns - use caution
code: SurvMetaSummary(gbm$meta) # Also show all numeric columns SurvMetaSummary(gbm$meta, show_all = TRUE)

what: 4.1 Basic survival plot - single gene, TCGA mode
title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: simplest call: one gene, faceting, median split. Samples split High (>= median) Low (< median) expression groups. log-rank p-value shown plot. sidecar CSV recording cutoff, group sizes, p-value written alongside PDF.
code: SurvPlot(   tcga_project = "TCGA-GBM",   genes        = "YAP1",   time_col     = "OS",   status_col   = "status",   output_file  = "~/Desktop/GBM_Survival/YAP1_survival.pdf" )

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: Pass character vector genes. gene produces separate PDF output directory. put genes single multi-page PDF, use path ending .pdf:
code: SurvPlot(   tcga_project = "TCGA-GBM",   genes        = c("YAP1", "CD276", "CD47", "S100A4", "CCL5"),   time_col     = "OS",   status_col   = "status",   output_file  = "~/Desktop/GBM_Survival/"   # directory -> one PDF per gene ) SurvPlot(   tcga_project = "TCGA-GBM",   genes        = c("YAP1", "CD276", "CD47"),   time_col     = "OS",   status_col   = "status",   output_file  = "~/Desktop/GBM_Survival/combined.pdf"  # single file )

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: facet_row facet_col arrange panels grid. one SurvPlot’s powerful features: see whether survival association holds within molecular subtype sex. One page produced per gene. grid sized automatically (~4.5 per panel). Facet cells fewer 2 * min_group_n samples left blank preserve alignment.
code: # Sex (rows) x IDH status (columns) SurvPlot(   tcga_project = "TCGA-GBM",   genes        = c("YAP1", "S100A4"),   time_col     = "OS",   status_col   = "status",   facet_row    = "gender",   facet_col    = "paper_IDH.status",   output_file  = "~/Desktop/GBM_Survival/" )

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: split_per_group = TRUE (default) computes median independently within facet cell - subgroup uniformly low expression still split 50/50. approach used published survival analyses. Set split_per_group = FALSE single global threshold.
code: SurvPlot(   tcga_project = "TCGA-GBM",   genes        = "YAP1",   split_method = "median",   time_col     = "OS",   status_col   = "status",   output_file  = "~/Desktop/GBM_Survival/" )

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: Produces four curves (Q1-Q4). Useful detecting non-linear dose-response relationships.
code: SurvPlot(   tcga_project     = "TCGA-GBM",   genes            = c("YAP1", "CD276", "CD47"),   split_method     = "quartile",   quartile_palette = c("#2166AC", "#92C5DE", "#F4A582", "#D6604D"),   time_col         = "OS",   status_col       = "status",   output_file      = "~/Desktop/GBM_Survival/quartiles.pdf" )

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: Split user-defined threshold. Use split_value = 0 gene-set enrichment scores 0 means enrichment.
code: SurvPlot(   tcga_project = "TCGA-GBM",   genes        = "YAP1",   split_method = "value",   split_value  = 4.0,    # log2 TPM+1 threshold   time_col     = "OS",   status_col   = "status",   output_file  = "~/Desktop/GBM_Survival/" )

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: Finds threshold maximizes log-rank statistic (survminer). Best used validation cohorts; can overfit small datasets.
code: SurvPlot(   tcga_project = "TCGA-GBM",   genes        = "YAP1",   split_method = "optimal",   time_col     = "OS",   status_col   = "status",   output_file  = "~/Desktop/GBM_Survival/" )

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: Instead individual genes, score sample mean expression named gene set. Pass named list gene_lists. element becomes one survival panel. enrichment-style interpretation, use split_value = 0 (score >= 0 means enriched):
code: SurvPlot(   tcga_project = "TCGA-LGG",   gene_lists   = list(     YAP_Activated = c("PRC1", "HPCA", "SRSF7", "TIMM17A", "PIM1"),     YAP_Repressed = c("ZIC1", "ZIC2", "CHD7", "KIF5C", "DPYSL2")   ),   split_method  = "median",   time_col      = "OS",   status_col    = "status",   facet_col     = "paper_Pan.Glioma.RNA.Expression.Cluster",   output_file   = "~/Desktop/LGG_Signatures/" ) SurvPlot(   tcga_project = "TCGA-LGG",   gene_lists   = list(     YAP_Activated = c("PRC1", "HPCA", "SRSF7"),     YAP_Repressed = c("ZIC1", "ZIC2", "CHD7")   ),   split_method = "value",   split_value  = 0,   time_col     = "OS",   status_col   = "status",   output_file  = "~/Desktop/LGG_Signatures/" )

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: Pass expression matrix metadata. Rownames meta must match colnames expr_mat.
code: # Cavalli medulloblastoma dataset (example) Cav.EXP  <- t(read.table("Cavalli_expression.txt", header = TRUE, row.names = 1)) Cav.meta <- read.table("Cavalli_pheno.txt", header = TRUE, row.names = 1)  res <- SurvPlot(   expr_mat     = Cav.EXP,   meta         = Cav.meta,   genes        = c("YAP1", "CD276", "CD47", "CCL5"),   time_col     = "survival",   # column name in your metadata   status_col   = "status",   facet_row    = "Gender",   facet_col    = "Subgroup",   split_method = "median",   output_file  = "~/Desktop/Cavalli_Survival/" )

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: Supply multivariate_covariates run multivariate Cox proportional hazards model alongside KM analysis. forest plot (hazard ratios + 95% CI) saved companion _cox.pdf file. Cox model uses continuous expression values, High/Low labels.
code: SurvPlot(   tcga_project            = "TCGA-GBM",   genes                   = c("YAP1", "S100A4"),   time_col                = "OS",   status_col              = "status",   facet_col               = "paper_IDH.status",   multivariate_covariates = c("gender", "age_at_index", "paper_IDH.status"),   output_file             = "~/Desktop/GBM_Survival/" )

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
code: SurvPlot(   tcga_project = "TARGET-NBL",   genes        = c("MYCN", "ALK"),   time_col     = "OS",   status_col   = "status",   split_method = "optimal",   output_file  = "~/Desktop/NBL_Survival/" )

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: SurvPlot returns list $plots $sidecar. sidecar records every panel’s cutoff, group sizes, log-rank p-value - complete audit trail stratification used. sidecar CSV columns:
code: res <- SurvPlot(   tcga_project = "TCGA-GBM",   genes        = c("YAP1", "CD276"),   time_col     = "OS",   status_col   = "status" )  # Inspect the sidecar head(res$sidecar)  # Access a ggsurvplot object for further customization res$plots[["YAP1"]][[1]]$plot

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
code: library(scSidekick)  # Step 1: download and cache gbm <- LoadTCGA("TCGA-GBM")  # Step 2: enrich with molecular subtypes and full clinical data gbm <- EnrichTCGAMeta(gbm)  # Step 3: explore available metadata columns SurvMetaSummary(gbm$meta)  # Step 4: run survival analysis SurvPlot(   expr_mat                = gbm$expr_mat,   meta                    = gbm$meta,   genes                   = c("YAP1", "S100A4", "CD276", "CD47"),   time_col                = "OS",   status_col              = "status",   facet_row               = "gender",   facet_col               = "paper_IDH.status",   split_method            = "median",   multivariate_covariates = c("gender", "age_at_index", "paper_IDH.status"),   output_file             = "~/Desktop/GBM_Survival/" )

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
text: Vignette 08: RunGSEA - pathway enrichment analysis single-cell data Vignette 09: RunGSEA_pseudobulk - sample-level pseudobulk GSEA Vignette 10: PlotPathwayButterfly - uses mean-expression scorer single-cell pathway state mapping

title: scSidekick - Survival Analysis: TCGA & Custom Cohorts
code: sessionInfo()

title: scSidekick - Co-expression Analysis
text: Three functions cover co-expression workflow end--end: CorrelateGene() - correlate one gene interest every gene, within cell type, single-cell, pseudobulk, group-average resolution. PlotCorrelation() - visualize results per-cell-type volcano-style plots (r vs. -log10 adjusted p-value). SplitByGene() - label cell high / low (expressed / zero) based gene’s expression, per cell type. Feeds directly RunGSEA_pseudobulk() FindMarkers().

title: scSidekick - Co-expression Analysis
text: default level = "sc" computes Spearman correlation across individual cells within cell type. genes expressed least min_pct% cells given cell type tested (expressed_only = TRUE default). row one gene one cell type. Key columns:
code: cor_sc <- CorrelateGene(bmcite,   gene     = "LYZ",   group.by = "celltype.l1",   level    = "sc")  head(cor_sc)

title: scSidekick - Co-expression Analysis
text: PlotCorrelation() draws one panel per cell type showing top n_pos positively top n_neg negatively correlated genes. Significant genes (padj < padj_cutoff) colored; top label_n direction labeled. Panels assembled patchwork::wrap_plots(). Restrict specific cell types cell_type: Save PDF (.legend sidecar written automatically):
code: PlotCorrelation(cor_sc,   n_pos       = 20,   n_neg       = 20,   label_n     = 5,   padj_cutoff = 0.05,   r_cutoff    = 0.3,     # draws ±0.3 dotted reference lines   ncol        = 3) PlotCorrelation(cor_sc,   cell_type = c("NK", "Mono"),   n_pos     = 15,   n_neg     = 15,   label_n   = 6) PlotCorrelation(cor_sc,   n_pos      = 20,   n_neg      = 20,   output_dir = "./Figures",   file_name  = "LYZ_coexpression")

title: scSidekick - Co-expression Analysis
text: multi-donor datasets, pseudobulk correlation (level = "pseudobulk") aggregates counts donor x cell-type level first. removes within-donor cell-count inflation appropriate multi-sample comparisons. Requires sample_column.
code: cor_pb <- CorrelateGene(bmcite,   gene          = "LYZ",   group.by      = "celltype.l1",   level         = "pseudobulk",   sample_column = "donor",   min_donors    = 3)

title: scSidekick - Co-expression Analysis
text: level = "group" averages expression within combination cell type group_column, correlates across group-level averages. Useful donors groups many (e.g. comparing many cell-type x timepoint combinations).
code: cor_grp <- CorrelateGene(bmcite,   gene         = "LYZ",   group.by     = "celltype.l1",   level        = "group",   group_column = "donor",   min_groups   = 3)

title: scSidekick - Co-expression Analysis
text: Pass compare_by run correlation separately within level grouping variable (e.g. sex, condition, batch). result gains compare_group column, PlotCorrelation() automatically lays one column per comparison group.
code: cor_by_donor <- CorrelateGene(bmcite,   gene       = "LYZ",   group.by   = "celltype.l1",   level      = "sc",   compare_by = "donor")  # ncol defaults to the number of comparison groups PlotCorrelation(cor_by_donor,   cell_type = "Mono",   n_pos     = 15,   n_neg     = 15)

what: 5. SplitByGene() - label cells for downstream analysis
title: scSidekick - Co-expression Analysis
text: SplitByGene() adds new metadata column splitting cells expression groups within cell type. column plugs straight RunGSEA_pseudobulk() (contrast.) Seurat::FindMarkers().

previous_headings: 5. SplitByGene() - label cells for downstream analysis
title: scSidekick - Co-expression Analysis
code: bmcite <- SplitByGene(bmcite,   gene     = "LYZ",   group.by = "celltype.l1",   split    = "zero_vs_expressed")  # New column "LYZ_split" is now in meta.data: table(bmcite$LYZ_split, bmcite$celltype.l1, useNA = "ifany")

previous_headings: 5. SplitByGene() - label cells for downstream analysis
title: scSidekick - Co-expression Analysis
text: Middle cells (two quantiles) receive NA excluded downstream tests, keeping contrast extremes.
code: bmcite <- SplitByGene(bmcite,   gene           = "LYZ",   group.by       = "celltype.l1",   split          = "quantile",   quantile_range = c(0.25, 0.75))  # bottom 25% = "low", top 25% = "high"

previous_headings: 5. SplitByGene() - label cells for downstream analysis
title: scSidekick - Co-expression Analysis
code: RunGSEA_pseudobulk(bmcite,   group.by    = "celltype.l1",   sample.by   = "donor",   contrast.by = "LYZ_split",   contrasts   = list(expressed_vs_zero = "expressed - zero"),   species     = "human",   output_dir  = "./Figures/LYZ_pseudobulk_GSEA")

previous_headings: 5. SplitByGene() - label cells for downstream analysis
title: scSidekick - Co-expression Analysis
code: mono <- subset(bmcite, celltype.l1 == "Mono")  markers <- Seurat::FindMarkers(mono,   group.by  = "LYZ_split",   ident.1   = "expressed",   ident.2   = "zero",   min.pct   = 0.1,   logfc.threshold = 0.25)  head(markers)

title: scSidekick - Co-expression Analysis
text: Vignette 05: GroupHeatmap(), SplitDotPlot() - expression summaries per cell type Vignette 08: RunGSEA() - pathway analysis gene list Vignette 09: RunGSEA_pseudobulk() - full pseudobulk GSEA pipeline (contrast.accepts column SplitByGene)

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
text: Distinguishing malignant cells normal stromal immune infiltrates common challenge tumor single-cell studies. scSidekick provides two complementary wrappers: RunInferCNV() - full inferCNV workflow automatic gene-order generation, downsampling, HMM-optional inference, Tirosh-style reference-anchored CNV score threshold. RunCopyKAT() - fast Bayesian CNV calling via CopyKAT; can run unsupervised mode (normal reference required). functions add new metadata columns Seurat object save standard figures output_dir.

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
text: Run compare CNV_Prediction vs. copykat_prediction maximum confidence.

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
code: # inferCNV (Bioconductor) if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager")) install.packages("BiocManager") BiocManager::install("infercnv")  # CopyKAT (GitHub) devtools::install_github("navinlabcode/copykat")

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
text: RunInferCNV() automatically runs AnnotateFeatures() generate gene order table object annotated yet.
code: library(scSidekick) library(Seurat)  # Typical tumor dataset: immune and stromal cells serve as the normal reference obj <- RunInferCNV(obj,   cell_type_col   = "Assignment",      # metadata column with cell-type labels   ref_group_names = c("T cell", "B cell", "Macrophage", "Endothelial"),   output_dir      = "./Figures/inferCNV",   species         = "human")

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
text: Three PDFs saved output_dir: CNV_proportion_heatmap.pdf - ComplexHeatmap per-chromosome CNV proportions, split cell type. CNV_dimplot.pdf - UMAP colored CNV_Prediction (left) cell type (right). CNV_score_featureplot.pdf - Continuous CNV_score overlaid UMAP.

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
text: default cnv_score_threshold = "auto" anchors threshold reference cells: cnv_k = 2 (default) good starting point. Increase call fewer cells malignant (conservative); decrease sensitive. Override fixed numeric value biological reason: Inspect score distribution choose threshold:
code: threshold = mean(ref_scores) + cnv_k * sd(ref_scores) # More conservative: require 3 SD above reference mean obj <- RunInferCNV(obj,   cell_type_col   = "Assignment",   ref_group_names = c("T cell", "B cell", "Macrophage"),   output_dir      = "./Figures/inferCNV",   species         = "human",   cnv_k           = 3) obj <- RunInferCNV(obj,   cell_type_col       = "Assignment",   ref_group_names     = c("T cell", "B cell"),   output_dir          = "./Figures/inferCNV",   species             = "human",   cnv_score_threshold = 0.02) hist(obj$CNV_score,   breaks = 50,   main   = "CNV score distribution",   xlab   = "CNV score",   col    = "steelblue") abline(v = 0.02, col = "red", lty = 2)  # Tabulate predictions table(obj$CNV_Prediction, obj$Assignment)

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
text: inferCNV can take hours large datasets. Use resume = TRUE first run skip inference step jump straight post-processing (useful tuning cnv_k cnv_score_threshold):
code: # First run - full inference (~2 hours) obj <- RunInferCNV(obj,   cell_type_col   = "Assignment",   ref_group_names = c("T cell", "B cell"),   output_dir      = "./Figures/inferCNV",   species         = "human")  # Tune threshold without re-running - post-processing only (~seconds) obj <- RunInferCNV(obj,   cell_type_col       = "Assignment",   ref_group_names     = c("T cell", "B cell"),   output_dir          = "./Figures/inferCNV",   species             = "human",   cnv_score_threshold = 0.015,   resume              = TRUE)

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
code: RunInferCNV(obj,   cell_type_col   = "Assignment",   ref_group_names = c("T cell", "B cell"),   output_dir      = "./Figures/inferCNV_HMM",   species         = "human",   HMM             = TRUE,     # adds subclonal CNV state calls; much slower   denoise         = TRUE,   downsample      = 300L,     # cells per group (NULL = use all)   cutoff          = 0.1,      # 0.1 for 10x; use 1 for Smart-seq2   num_threads     = 8L,   caffeinate      = TRUE)     # keep laptop awake during long run

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
text: default autosomes + X/Y analyzed. Restrict subset prior reason (e.g., known focal amplification chr8q MYC-driven tumors):
code: RunInferCNV(obj,   cell_type_col   = "Assignment",   ref_group_names = c("T cell", "B cell"),   output_dir      = "./Figures/inferCNV",   species         = "human",   chromosomes     = as.character(c(1:8, 17)))

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
code: obj <- RunCopyKAT(obj,   cell_type_col = "Assignment",   ref_cells     = c("T cell", "B cell", "Macrophage"),   output_dir    = "./Figures/CopyKAT",   n_cores       = 8L)

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
text: sample contains confidently normal cells, omit ref_cells: CopyKAT infer diploid baseline automatically.
code: obj <- RunCopyKAT(obj,   cell_type_col = "Assignment",   output_dir    = "./Figures/CopyKAT",   n_cores       = 8L)

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
text: ".defined" cells CopyKAT confidently classify - typically sparse cells cells boundary two populations.

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
text: Use require agreement high-confidence malignancy calls: Visualize agreement:
code: # Run both (output_dir can be the same or different) obj <- RunInferCNV(obj,   cell_type_col   = "Assignment",   ref_group_names = c("T cell", "B cell"),   output_dir      = "./Figures/inferCNV",   species         = "human")  obj <- RunCopyKAT(obj,   cell_type_col = "Assignment",   ref_cells     = c("T cell", "B cell"),   output_dir    = "./Figures/CopyKAT")  # Cells called malignant/aneuploid by BOTH tools obj$CNV_consensus <- with(obj@meta.data,   ifelse(CNV_Prediction == "Malignant" & copykat_prediction == "aneuploid",          "Malignant", "Normal/Uncertain"))  table(obj$CNV_consensus, obj$Assignment) library(ggplot2)  ggplot(obj@meta.data, aes(x = CNV_score, y = copykat_score,                             color = CNV_consensus)) +   geom_point(size = 0.5, alpha = 0.6) +   scale_color_manual(values = c(Malignant = "#ab3000",                                  "Normal/Uncertain" = "#2f4b7c")) +   labs(x = "inferCNV score", y = "CopyKAT score",        title = "CNV score concordance") +   theme_classic()

title: scSidekick - Copy Number Variation Inference
text: AnnotateFeatures() / GetGeneOrder() - gene-order annotation used internally RunInferCNV() (vignette 01) PlotDimPlots() - visualize CNV_Prediction UMAP inference (vignette 03) RunWilcoxAUC() - find markers distinguishing malignant normal clusters labeling

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: Consensus non-negative matrix factorization (cNMF; Kotliar et al. 2019, eLife) discovers gene expression programs (GEPs): recurrent modules co-expressed genes can correspond cell-type identity shared activities cell cycle, stress, interferon response. Unlike clustering, cell can use several programs , makes cNMF natural complement annotation, pathway, co-expression tools elsewhere scSidekick. scSidekick wraps Python cNMF package reticulate three functions: RunCNMF() - prepares data, runs heavy factorization (optionally across parallel workers Harmony batch correction), writes K-selection diagnostic. GetCNMFPrograms() - extracts consensus result chosen K attaches per-cell usages dimensional reduction (metadata) plus gene spectra object. GetCNMFTopGenes() - pulls top genes per program heatmaps downstream enrichment. cNMF interactive design: run factorization, inspect K-selection plot, choose number programs, extract. single one-shot call without compromising science. cNMF Python tool. R dependency. Section 1 walks one-time environment setup captures every compatibility fix needed drive R; part likely trip , carefully .

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: cNMF dependencies Python packages compiled extensions. Driving R reticulate (runs Python embedded R process) exposes several version-compatibility issues. recipe known-good combination. Run .

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
code: # Python 3.9 from conda-forge conda create -n cnmf_env python=3.9 -c conda-forge -y conda activate cnmf_env

what: 1.2 Install cNMF and pin the compatible versions
title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: line addresses specific failure mode (noted comment). Run order. R side, make sure reticulate installed (reasonably current): Alternative pinning NumPy: current reticulate (>= ~1.39) can handle NumPy 2.0, scanpy’s seurat_v3 HVG step (used batch correction) still needs compiled scikit-misc match installed NumPy. Pinning numpy<2 keeps whole stack consistent simplest reliable path.
code: # (a) Provide libc++ INSIDE the env. numba/llvmlite (pulled in by scanpy) load a #     compiled dylib that needs libc++.1.dylib; under R/reticulate the system #     copy is not on the loader path, so install it into the env. conda install -c conda-forge libcxx -y  # (b) Install cNMF itself. pip install cnmf  # (c) Pin NumPy < 2. Two things require this: (i) reticulate's R<->NumPy bridge #     (older reticulate cannot convert arrays from NumPy 2.0), and (ii) the #     scikit-misc binary used by scanpy's seurat_v3 highly-variable-gene step. pip install "numpy<2"  # (d) ONLY needed for batch correction (Section 7): harmonypy with the API that #     cNMF's Preprocess expects. Newer harmonypy (0.0.10+) removed the internal #     attribute cNMF reads, so pin 0.0.9. pip install "harmonypy==0.0.9" install.packages("reticulate")

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: reticulate can initialize one Python interpreter per R session, binding must happen Python-touching code runs. robust approach set RETICULATE_PYTHON top fresh session: (Find path conda run -n cnmf_env python -c "import sys; print(sys.executable)", typically ~/anaconda3/envs/cnmf_env/bin/python / ~/miniconda3/envs/cnmf_env/bin/python.)
code: # FIRST line of a fresh R session, before library() calls: Sys.setenv(RETICULATE_PYTHON = "/path/to/anaconda3/envs/cnmf_env/bin/python")  library(scSidekick) library(Seurat)

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: py_config() shows wrong interpreter, restart R set RETICULATE_PYTHON anything else. See Troubleshooting table end specific errors may hit fixes.
code: reticulate::py_config()                  # should show .../cnmf_env/bin/python, numpy 1.x reticulate::py_module_available("cnmf")  # must be TRUE

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: RunCNMF() writes raw-counts AnnData, runs cNMF prepare -> factorize -> combine -> k_selection_plot. Point environment conda_env (rely RETICULATE_PYTHON Section 1.3). Runtime scales n_iter * length(k_range), object size (defaults 100 x 11 = 1100 NMF fits). quick end--end test, shrink :
code: Immune <- RunCNMF(Immune,   output_dir = "./Figures/cNMF",   k_range    = 5:15,     # candidate numbers of programs   n_iter     = 100,      # NMF replicates per K (robustness)   conda_env  = "cnmf_env") Immune <- RunCNMF(Immune, output_dir = "./Figures/cNMF",                   n_iter = 5, k_range = 5:8, conda_env = "cnmf_env")

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: factorize step parallelizes across worker processes. Set n_workers number cores can spare: worker logs <output_dir>/<name>.factorize.worker_<>.log - tail -f one watch progress.
code: Immune <- RunCNMF(Immune, output_dir = "./Figures/cNMF",                   k_range = 5:15, n_iter = 100,                   n_workers = 6,           # 6 parallel cNMF factorize workers                   conda_env = "cnmf_env")

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: Reusing name output_dir makes cNMF treat existing factorization files done. clean run different parameters, pass fresh name (e.g. name = "Immune_k5to15").

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: RunCNMF() writes K-selection plot (stability vs. reconstruction error) , show_plot = TRUE (default), renders RStudio Plots pane. Pick K stability peak / error elbow. honest way automate choice; inspect diagnostic decide.
code: # The plot is at <output_dir>/cnmf/cnmf.k_selection.png and is shown # automatically. To view it again later: magick::image_read("./Figures/cNMF/cnmf/cnmf.k_selection.png")

what: 4. Extracting programs for a chosen K
title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: GetCNMFPrograms() runs consensus step chosen k density_threshold (outlier filter; lower stricter, 2.0 keeps everything), attaches results object. adds: dimensional reduction Immune[["cnmf"]] embeddings cNMF_1 ... cNMF_k (per-cell program usages), can overlay programs UMAP. Metadata columns cNMF_1 ... cNMF_k (add_metadata = TRUE). Immune@misc$cnmf$results holding usage, spectra_scores (gene loadings), spectra_tpm, top_genes. consensus clustergram written next outputs shown Plots pane. RunCNMF() GetCNMFPrograms() also write analysis_params.json output_dir (provenance sidecar produced RunGSEA(), RunCellChat(), etc.). records cNMF parameters draft methods paragraph, picked automatically create_analysis_pptx():
code: Immune <- GetCNMFPrograms(Immune,   k                 = 10,      # chosen from the K-selection plot   density_threshold = 0.1,   conda_env         = "cnmf_env") jsonlite::read_json(file.path("./Figures/cNMF", "analysis_params.json"))$methods_text

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: usages live numeric metadata, standard scSidekick plotting functions accept “features” directly.

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
code: PlotFeaturePlots(Immune,   features   = paste0("cNMF_", 1:10),   output_dir = "./Figures/cNMF/usage_maps")

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
code: GroupHeatmap(Immune,   features         = paste0("cNMF_", 1:10),   group.by         = c("Group", "Sex"),   column_split_by  = "genotype")

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: GetCNMFTopGenes() returns tidy program / gene / rank table feeds straight blocked GroupHeatmap (SplitDotPlot, RunGSEA, RunEnrichment): Restrict specific programs programs: (n capped top_n_genes set GetCNMFPrograms(), default 50.)
code: tg <- GetCNMFTopGenes(Immune, n = 15)        # top 15 genes per program head(tg)  # Heatmap of program marker genes, blocked by program: GroupHeatmap(Immune,   features      = tg$gene,   group.by      = "CellType",   feature_split = stats::setNames(tg$program, tg$gene)) tg <- GetCNMFTopGenes(Immune, n = 20, programs = c(1, 3, 5))

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: RunEnrichment() accepts GetCNMFTopGenes() table directly, running enrichR program’s top genes one call. Note: RunEnrichment() requires library(enrichR) called current session. enrichR initializes connection .onAttach(), fires library(), requireNamespace().
code: library(enrichR)  tg <- GetCNMFTopGenes(Immune, n = 50)   # use top 50 for enrichment  RunEnrichment(   deg_df       = tg,   gene_column  = "gene",   group_column = "program",   databases    = "MSigDB_Hallmark_2020",   output_dir   = "./Figures/cNMF/enrichment")

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
code: # 1. Factorize (parallel), then inspect the K-selection plot Immune <- RunCNMF(Immune, output_dir = "./Figures/cNMF",                   k_range = 5:15, n_iter = 100, n_workers = 6,                   conda_env = "cnmf_env", caffeinate = TRUE)  # 2. Choose K from the plot, extract the consensus programs Immune <- GetCNMFPrograms(Immune, k = 10, density_threshold = 0.1,                           conda_env = "cnmf_env")  # 3. Visualize PlotFeaturePlots(Immune, features = paste0("cNMF_", 1:10),                  output_dir = "./Figures/cNMF/usage_maps") tg <- GetCNMFTopGenes(Immune, n = 15) GroupHeatmap(Immune, features = tg$gene, group.by = "CellType",              feature_split = stats::setNames(tg$program, tg$gene))

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: default cNMF can learn programs reflect batch rather biology. cNMF’s recommended remedy (Kotliar et al., Figure 5) Harmony-correct counts factorization, learn usages corrected data, re-fit spectra uncorrected TP10K data. Supply one metadata columns batch.RunCNMF() runs workflow via cNMF’s Preprocess: Batch correction needs extra dependencies Section 1.2: numpy<2-consistent scikit-misc (seurat_v3 HVG step) harmonypy==0.0.9 (correction ). skipped , install restart R using batch.. batch.set, genes_use ignored (Preprocess selects high-variance genes corrected data), cNMF writes <name>.batchcorrect.Corrected.HVG.Varnorm.h5ad, *.TP10K.h5ad, *.Corrected.HVGs.txt, prepare consumes. Harmony correction slow. run crashes correction factorization, re-run resume = TRUE reuse corrected files skip straight factorization:
code: Immune <- RunCNMF(Immune,   output_dir = "./Figures/cNMF",   k_range    = 5:15,   n_iter     = 100,   batch.by   = "Sample",          # or c("Sex", "Sample")   conda_env  = "cnmf_env") Immune <- RunCNMF(Immune, output_dir = "./Figures/cNMF",                   batch.by = "Sample", k_range = 5:15,                   resume = TRUE, conda_env = "cnmf_env")

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: Every error environment / binary-compatibility issue, problem data wrapper. listed order likely hit . General rule: bind env first fresh R session, keep whole Python stack consistent numpy < 2.

title: scSidekick - Gene Expression Programs with cNMF
text: Vignette 06: CellTypeAssignmentHelper() - annotate cell types can relate programs Vignette 16: CorrelateGene() / PlotCorrelation() - gene co-expression, complementary view program structure Vignette 08: RunGSEA() - feed GetCNMFTopGenes() output gene set characterize program

title: scSidekick - Upstream Regulator Analysis
text: Differential expression tells genes change; upstream regulator analysis (URA) asks transcription factors drive changes. RunURA() infers per-sample TF activity gene expression using decoupleR CollecTRI TF-target network, (optionally) tests differential TF activity conditions. workflow: Collapse cells pseudobulk profiles (per group, donor, stratifier) - statistically sound replicates downstream testing. Score TF activity per sample decoupleR’s univariate linear model (ulm, default; viper mlm also available) CollecTRI. contrast given, test differential activity (limma least 3 donors per side, t-test otherwise). Save TF-activity heatmap differential bubble plot, .legend sidecar. RunURA() complements pathway tools (Vignettes 08-10): GSEA finds enriched gene sets, URA finds regulators upstream . decoupleR optional dependency. Install (Section 1). CollecTRI network fetched OmniPath first use cached locally.

title: scSidekick - Upstream Regulator Analysis
text: first RunURA() call downloads CollecTRI OmniPath caches ~/.scSidekick/collectri_<organism>.rds, subsequent runs offline instant. See note network access end OmniPath blocked network.
code: if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) install.packages("BiocManager") BiocManager::install("decoupleR") # Optional but recommended for the differential heatmap and limma-based testing: BiocManager::install(c("limma", "ComplexHeatmap")) library(scSidekick) library(Seurat)

title: scSidekick - Upstream Regulator Analysis
text: just group., RunURA() infers mean TF activity per group saves z-scored activity heatmap variable TFs. return value list:
code: ura <- RunURA(obj,   group.by   = "CellType",   organism   = "human",   output_dir = "./Figures/URA") ura$activity[1:5, 1:4]      # TFs x cell types

title: scSidekick - Upstream Regulator Analysis
text: Supply contrast.(condition column) contrast (c("test", "reference") pair), plus donor.enable replicate-based testing. produces differential heatmap (activity difference, dots marking p.adj < 0.05) bubble plot.
code: ura <- RunURA(obj,   group.by    = "CellType",   donor.by    = "Donor.ID",            # biological replicates -> limma   contrast.by = "Diagnosis",   contrast    = c("AD", "Control"),    # test vs reference   organism    = "human",   output_dir  = "./Figures/URA")  head(ura$de)   # TF, cell_type, activity_diff, p.value, p.adj, contrast ...

title: scSidekick - Upstream Regulator Analysis
code: ura <- RunURA(obj,   group.by    = "CellType",   donor.by    = "Donor.ID",   contrast.by = "Diagnosis",   contrast    = list(c("AD", "Control"), c("MCI", "Control")),   output_dir  = "./Figures/URA")

title: scSidekick - Upstream Regulator Analysis
text: split.(e.g. "Sex") adds second dimension: contrast computed within level, columns heatmap grouped , bubble plot faceted.
code: ura <- RunURA(obj,   group.by    = "CellType",   donor.by    = "Donor.ID",   split.by    = "Sex",   contrast.by = "Diagnosis",   contrast    = c("AD", "Control"),   output_dir  = "./Figures/URA")

title: scSidekick - Upstream Regulator Analysis
text: use_pseudobulk = TRUE (default) rigorous route: TF activity inferred per-sample pseudobulk averages. use_pseudobulk = FALSE, decoupleR scores every cell directly activities averaged per group (simpler, donor.needed, cells pseudoreplicates interpret DE p-values caution; use max_cells subsample large objects). force = TRUE recomputes pseudobulk even cached result stored obj@misc$pseudobulk. custom network (data frame source, target, mor) can passed instead CollecTRI.
code: RunURA(obj,   group.by       = "CellType",   contrast.by    = "Diagnosis",   contrast       = c("AD", "Control"),   method         = "ulm",        # "ulm" (default), "viper", or "mlm"   n_top          = 30,           # TFs shown in plots   min_cells      = 10,           # minimum cells per pseudobulk group   group.levels   = c("Microglia", "Astrocyte"),  # restrict to some groups   use_pseudobulk = TRUE,         # FALSE = score per cell, then average   organism       = "human",      # or "mouse"   output_dir     = "./Figures/URA")

title: scSidekick - Upstream Regulator Analysis
text: output_dir set, RunURA() writes (file names encode group., split., contrast): <object>_URA_by_<group>..._heatmap.pdf - one per contrast; rows top TFs, columns cell types (grouped split.), color activity difference, black dots mark p.adj < 0.05. <object>_URA_by_<group>..._bubble.pdf - matrix bubble plot (cell type x TF, color = activity difference, size = -log10 p.adj), faceted contrast split.. .legend sidecar next PDF, create_analysis_pptx() picks description automatically.

title: scSidekick - Upstream Regulator Analysis
text: CollecTRI fetched OmniPath web service first use. RunURA() tries several fallbacks (bundled copy, alternate OmniPathR options, disk cache, direct REST download) caches working network ~/.scSidekick/collectri_<organism>.rds. institution blocks OmniPath, fetch network unrestricted machine drop cache: every RunURA() call uses cached network offline. can also pass directly via network argument.
code: net <- decoupleR::get_collectri(organism = "human") saveRDS(net, "~/.scSidekick/collectri_human.rds")

title: scSidekick - Upstream Regulator Analysis
text: Vignette 08-10: RunGSEA() pathway family - enriched gene sets URA finds regulators Vignette 14: ComputePseudobulk() - donor-level aggregation URA builds Vignette 12: create_analysis_pptx() - assembles URA figures (.legend text) deck
